title: Enhanced Impact Mitigation via 3D-Multilayered Material Architectures
authors: Thomas Butruille, Joshua C. Crone, Carlos M. Portela
abstract: Materials designed by nature commonly exhibit functional grading and laminated structures, particularly when intended for enhanced impact protection. Synthetic materials have also found success in exploiting this concept with fully dense but spatially varying architectures, as is the case with advanced fiber-based composites. In the lightweight materials space, porous architected materials have shown benefits for extreme impact mitigation, proving to be advantageous in dissipating large amounts of energy per unit mass, but rarely harness the benefits of layering or functional grading in designs. Here, a design paradigm for lightweight multilayered materials towards high impact-mitigation efficacy is demonstrated, showing that the use of alternating monolithic and beam-based architectures leads to enhanced and predictable responses under extreme conditions. These layered, mass-equivalent `heterostructures' with different ordering and proportions of octet and monolithic layers outperform single-architecture lattices on a mass-normalized energy dissipation basis by >50% when subjected to supersonic microparticle impact. Through analysis that combines wave-propagation analysis, nonlinear finite element simulations, and post-impact crater reconstruction, layer-by-layer mechanical properties are mapped to crater formation and energy dissipation behaviors. This heterostructure design framework offers a simple approach towards tuning failure and impact resistance of materials for protective applications from Whipple shields to sports equipment.
summary: This paper demonstrates that 3D-printed multilayered 'heterostructures'—alternating between solid and lattice architectures—provide significantly better protection against high-velocity impacts than single-material designs. By using a combination of experimental particle impact testing and numerical modeling, the authors show that these layers can be programmed to recruit more material into the impact zone to dissipate energy. This approach offers a scalable framework for designing advanced protective equipment like Whipple shields or sports gear.
main_problem: Developing lightweight, high-efficiency 3D-architected materials for extreme impact mitigation by moving beyond single-architecture designs toward functional grading and layering.
main_result: Heterostructured shields consisting of alternating monolithic and lattice layers outperform single-architecture lattices by over 50% in mass-normalized energy dissipation due to enhanced mass recruitment and crack-pinning.
method: The study uses Laser-Induced Particle Impact Testing (LIPIT) with supersonic microparticles, X-ray Computed Tomography (XCT) for crater reconstruction, and finite element simulations combined with analytical wave-propagation modeling.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of 3D-printed micro-scale heterostructures made of IP-Dip photoresist, featuring alternating layers of octet-truss lattices and monolithic (solid) polymer plates.
key_observables: Energy dissipation (mass-normalized), crater morphology (volume, area, radius), particle velocity (initial and final), and lateral stress-wave propagation.
important_parameters: Supersonic impact velocities (250-900 m/s), high strain rates (10^5 to 10^7 s^-1), relative density (approx. 33%), and micro-scale unit cell dimensions (approx. 6.7 micrometers).
main_assumptions: Assumes plastic incompressibility for monolithic layers, affine compressibility for octet lattices, and that the arrival of a plastic stress wave correlates directly with crater boundaries.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the design framework and experimental setup; Figure 2 compares impact events and architectures; Figure 3 presents energy dissipation metrics; Figure 4 shows XCT crater profiles; Figure 6 and 7 illustrate lateral stress-wave propagation and crater boundaries.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the design paradigm of heterostructures, describes the LIPIT experimental platform and fabrication, presents results on energy dissipation and mass recruitment, analyzes stress-wave propagation through analytical and simulation methods, and validates wave speeds using uniaxial compression data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: a) Design framework for heterostructured suspended shields, depicting 4Monolithic (M)-1Octet (O), 1O-4M, 1M-4O, 4O-1M, and 1M-1O-1M-2O architectures and their respective mass ratios by architecture, including a base layer. b) Laser-induced microparticle impact of a suspended lattice and c) monolithic, octet, and tetrakaidecahedron lattices discretized in mass-equivalent layers (nL), dictated by shield thickness or tesselation, with n ranging from 4 to 10.

Figure 2: a) Multilayered octet suspended lattices prior to impact, and b) high-speed imaging frames from microparticle impact events at four, six, eight, and ten unit cell thickness (including the base layer), with impact velocities of 367, 562, 792, and 775 m s-1 respectively. c) Post-impact SEM of octet lattice craters across shield thicknesses. d-f) Best-fit dimensional analysis relation between select dimensionless groups for octet, tetrakaidecahedron, and monolithic suspended shields across shield thicknesses. Scale bars, 100 µm.
Figure 3: Incident particle velocity vs crater-mass-normalized energy dissipation for all suspended shield architectures, assuming craters with constant area through thickness. Scale bars, 100 µm.

Figure 4: a) Crater profiles for tested shields, presenting crater radii normalized by the particle radius. XCT-enabled 3D reconstruction of representative post-impact suspended shields for b)5O, c) 4O-1M, d) 1O-4M, e) 1M-1O-1M-2O, f) 1M-4O, g) 4M-1O, and h) 5M architectures, respectively—including a cross-section view of the crater along with a top-down SEM micrograph and a 3D representation of the crater volume. Scale bars, 100 µm.

Figure 5: Normalized equivalent thickness versus normalized equivalent monolithic volume for all suspended shield architectures imaged with XCT. Cross-sections of select heterostructures at the particle impact crater location depicted at three layers and six layers of crater depth.
